title: Typed scene-graph data model with JSON serialization
short_description: Implement the Project/Scene/Layer/Node hierarchy with typed node subclasses, stable unique IDs, transforms, style roles, z-order, visibility windows, and the versioned JSON serialization that is a public contract for inspect/diff/verify.
long_description_md:
# Typed scene-graph data model with JSON serialization

## Why this task exists

The scene graph is anima-ai's single source of truth. Authored Python constructs it, the timeline compiler enriches it, the geometry resolver evaluates it, the renderer draws it, and the verifier inspects it. This shared representation is the architectural reason semantic verification can remain consistent with rendered output instead of becoming a separate approximation.

This task establishes that representation near the start of **Phase 1: Scene graph, timeline, and first pixels**. Every later Phase 1 subsystem depends on it: timeline tracks target stable node IDs, animation declarations mutate typed properties over time, geometry resolution dispatches by node type, and `anima inspect` exposes the serialized graph. Later roadmap phases extend the same model with style-pack assets, characters, map nodes, verification findings, and subtitles.

The JSON representation is not an internal convenience. It is a public, versioned interchange contract used by agents for inspection and diffing, by tests for deterministic snapshots, and eventually by `verify`, CLI consumers, and possible non-Python frontends. Identical graph inputs must therefore produce byte-stable canonical JSON, and incompatible schema changes must never be accepted silently.

This task also owns the public project entry-point convention: the shape of a project's `project.py` (how it constructs and exposes a `Project`, including the mandatory pinned style-pack reference field) is defined here, not reinvented by the CLI. `cli-framework`'s `anima new` scaffold consumes this convention when generating a runnable `project.py`, rather than the two tasks independently guessing at parallel, potentially incompatible shapes.

## Core model

Implement a fully typed public model under `anima` for:

- `Project`: project name, pinned style-pack reference (`id` and `version`), resolution (default 1920×1080), fps (default 30), project seed, narration WPM default (150), engine version, and an ordered sequence of scenes.
- `Scene`: stable ID, title, and an ordered layer stack. Scene duration remains derived by the timeline compiler and must not become an independently authored source of truth in this task.
- `Layer`: stable ID/name and an ordered collection of root nodes. Layer order is preserved as part of render order.
- Abstract `Node`: stable ID, containing layer ID, typed transform, optional style role, integer z-order, and typed visibility window.
- Typed value objects for two-dimensional vectors, resolution, style-pack references, transforms, and visibility windows. Transform data must cover position, rotation, scale, and anchor with documented deterministic defaults. Numeric values must be finite; resolution, fps, and WPM must be positive.

Built graph values must behave immutably so downstream verify/render operations cannot mutate the artifact they were given. Ordered relationships must use deterministic sequence semantics rather than sets or incidental mapping iteration order.

## IDs, hierarchy, and ordering invariants

IDs are agent-readable stable strings, never generated from memory addresses or collection positions. Empty or whitespace-only IDs are invalid. Scene IDs must be unique within a project, layer IDs unique within their scene, and node IDs unique across the entire project, including nodes recursively nested in groups. Reusing a node ID, inserting the same child under multiple parents, or placing a node under a layer that disagrees with its declared `layer` field must raise a structured graph-validation error naming the offending ID and model path. Collisions are errors; the library must never repair them by auto-suffixing or renaming.

Layer list order is the first render-order dimension. Within a layer or group, nodes order by ascending `z`, with their declared sequence order retained as the deterministic tie-breaker. The model must expose this ordering without requiring consumers to recreate it differently.

A visibility window represents the interval in which a node can participate in the scene. It supports unresolved/open boundaries needed before timeline compilation and validates resolved intervals as non-negative with end greater than or equal to start. Deriving entry and exit times from beats belongs to the timeline task; this task provides the typed field and serialization contract only.

`style_role` is an opaque pack-role reference at this stage. It must serialize faithfully, but resolving it against a real pack and flagging raw-color or missing-role violations belongs to the style-pack and verification phases.

## Phase 1 node subclasses

Ship real typed subclasses for the node kinds needed by Phase 1's first-pixels fixture:

- `Shape`, with typed geometry variants for the basic deterministic shapes selected by the implementation plan; mutually exclusive geometry must be represented as a discriminated union rather than a bag of optional properties.
- `Path`, with an ordered typed vector-path command representation rather than arbitrary dictionaries.
- `Text`, with content, font role, and size as required typed fields.
- `Group`, containing an ordered recursive sequence of child nodes. Child transforms remain local to the group; transform composition is implemented by the later geometry-resolver task.

Every subclass must be distinguishable through an explicit stable JSON discriminator such as `type`, and subtype payloads must contain only JSON-representable typed values. Do not use `dict[str, Any]`, Python class paths, pickling, or enum `repr` values as public payloads.

Asset, Character, BaseMap, Territory, Border, InvasionArrow, MapLabel, and MapMarker are deliberately excluded here: their real schemas belong to the style-pack and map phases. The discriminator/codec architecture must permit those types to be added through deliberate schema-version changes, but this task must not create placeholder subclasses with guessed fields.

## Versioned JSON contract

Provide public serialization and deserialization APIs for complete projects and their component values. The root object must contain a mandatory scene-graph `schema_version` independent of `engine_version`. Establish the first contract as `1.0`, document that additive compatible evolution increments the minor version and breaking changes increment the major version, and reject missing or unsupported versions with a typed error. No migration framework is required yet.

The v1 representation must use stable snake_case keys, explicit node-type discriminators, JSON primitives only, and arrays for all semantically ordered collections. Defaulted public fields must be emitted rather than omitted so inspection output is self-contained and diffs do not depend on constructor defaults. Non-finite floats are invalid. Unknown node discriminators, malformed subtype payloads, duplicate IDs, inconsistent layer membership, and unknown schema versions must fail with deterministic errors containing a machine-readable code and JSON/model path.

Canonical serialization must:

- preserve scene, layer, group-child, path-command, and equal-z sequence order;
- sort unordered object keys consistently;
- encode Unicode deterministically as UTF-8;
- produce byte-identical output for identical built graphs;
- round-trip `Project -> JSON -> Project` without semantic loss;
- reserialize decoded canonical JSON to the same bytes.

Check in a representative v1 contract fixture containing every Phase 1 node subtype, non-default transforms, multiple layers and z ties, open and resolved visibility windows, Unicode text, and a nested group. Snapshot tests must lock its exact shape and prevent accidental key renames, discriminator changes, default omission, or ordering drift. Also document the schema and compatibility policy for downstream `inspect`, diff, and verifier authors.

## Public API and downstream integration

Expose the intended authoring types from the documented public `anima` surface while keeping codec internals private or under a clearly named scene-graph module. Public types must pass mypy strict checking and support equality suitable for round-trip assertions. The timeline compiler, renderer, geometry resolver, and CLI are not implemented here; this task supplies the stable typed objects and codec those later tasks consume.

## Explicitly out of scope

- Script, Beat, Action, Track, Keyframe, easing, or duration compilation.
- Transform or visibility evaluation at a timestamp.
- Geometry resolution, rasterization, static-layer caching, preview generation, or video encoding.
- CLI command implementation, including `anima inspect`; the later CLI task calls this codec.
- Style-pack resolution, palette enforcement, bundled assets, characters, or paint rendering.
- Map-specific node types and map dataset references.
- VerificationReport/Finding schemas or conversion of model errors into Phase 2 findings.
roadmap_phase: Phase 1: Scene graph, timeline, and first pixels

deliverables.description: Typed, immutable Project/Scene/Layer/Node hierarchy and supporting value objects for resolution, pinned style-pack reference, vectors, transforms, and visibility windows, with documented defaults and validation for finite numeric values, positive project settings, valid intervals, and immutable ordered collections.

Phase 1 Shape, Path, Text, and recursive Group node subclasses with typed subtype payloads, project-wide node-ID uniqueness, layer-membership validation, collision errors without auto-renaming, and deterministic layer/z/sequence ordering.

Public schema-version 1.0 JSON codec with explicit node discriminators, canonical deterministic output, lossless round trips, strict malformed/unknown-version handling, and a checked-in representative contract fixture whose exact serialized form is snapshot-tested.

Strictly typed public anima exports and scene-graph contract documentation suitable for downstream timeline, inspect, renderer, and verifier consumers, with the complete scene-graph test suite passing lint and strict type checking.

verification.command: ruff check src/anima tests/scene_graph && mypy --strict src/anima && pytest -q tests/scene_graph
